data.title: Molina Healthcare: Looks Like A Sick Puppy
data.description:
Molina Healthcare (NYSE:MOH) is an upstart in the Health Insurance space. I last wrote about Molina in an article comparing them to United Healthcare (UNH) the old dog in the space.

At the time, I concluded that Molina probably offered the better valuation of the pair, and faced similar risks, however without the scale benefits of UNH.

Molina came out with earnings on October 22nd, and the results were terrible.

The company slashed guidance, cutting EPS estimates for the current year from $19 to $14. The problem was continued medical costs inflation. While much of the weakness in the Marketplace segment, this only accounted for half of the deterioration.

The continued slide in margins significantly reduces the credibility of the Molina outlook.

The market seems to agree with this. As at the time of writing, the Molina share price had dropped over 15%. Peer Centene (CNC) had dropped in sympathy by 4%. Elevance (ELV) had fared better, having reported Q3 already, while United Healthcare (UNH), also due to report, escaped pretty much unscathed.

Q3 Earnings and Outlook

Some level of negative response was inevitable following the big haircut in guidance. The quarter had some ominous figures.

Premium revenues were up around 11% year on year. However earnings per share shrunk to $1,51 compared to $5.65 for the comparable period, on a net income of $79m, down from $326m year on year.

The big driver of the miss was the medical care ratio, which came in at 92.6%, which shaved the after tax margin to a barely profitable 0.7% of revenues.

The company reported pressure in the medical care ratio in all segments, while pointing out that half of the deterioration came from the Marketplace segment, which accounted for just 10% of revenues. The MCR shift was dramatic from 85.4% to 95.6% for this segment.

This seems to be an attempt to pitch the problem as more easily addressable, by strong action in that one segment. However, the broad nature of the declines hints at a more pronounced utilisation, medical inflation, and pricing problem.

Of further concern is a negative cash flow print. Operating cash flow for the year to date was a negative of $237 million in contrast to the strong cash flow generation of $868m for the first 9 months of 2025. This is not due to cash being invested, but rather normal claims and Medicaid risk payments.

Investors would have hoped that the challenges had been fully recognised in Q2, when the previous earnings guidance was set due to the medical cost ratio coming in at 90.4%.

A full 2.2% worsening of the medical claims ratio points to real problems with the prior reserving assumptions.

At least Molina seems to be addressing the under-pricing in marketplace actively. This was discussed in an intense earnings call.

2026 rate increases averaged 30%, ranging from 15% to 45%. The company exited the most problematic counties, reducing the footprint by 20%. They expect premiums to reduce by half in 2026 as they cut exposure.

This provides a positive outlook, and management seemed confident of their $46bn premium projection for 2026, however, they did not provide detailed guidance on their expected medical cost ratio. They indicated that they hoped to bring the segment back to break-even.

The challenges in the Medicaid segment remain, with the medical cost ratio here still elevated. In my view, there was not enough detail given on management actions here to be confident that margins can be recovered.

Valuation

Valuations for the group are divided quite cleanly between the “Big Dogs” United and Elevance, and the “Pups” Molina and Centene.

Molina is the cheapest of the pack on a trailing price to earnings basis, sitting just under Centene at around 8x earnings.

Conclusion

The second difficult quarter in a row raises some serious questions about the persistent claims ratio creep. While price increases can be expected to help, this will be a drag on membership growth. A further concern is that the quality of the membership will be skewed by higher prices towards less healthy members. In this case price increases might not be effective in bringing down the medical cost ratio, and thus prove counter-productive to both top and bottom lines.

I am rating Molina as a hold until they are able to report a stabilised medical cost ratio.

Finally, the trends reported by Molina show a sharp decline in profitability. Looking forward to the upcoming earnings for UNH and CNC, investors should be cautious of similar trends pulling down their results. UNH has the buffer of PBM earnings, while CNC is more heavily exposed to insurance risk business. I’m taking a conservative position on UNH and particularly CNC going into earnings.

data.title: SCHG: A Simple, Low Cost Fund For Big Growth & Long-Term Power
data.description:
Introduction

In times like now, when the market continues to make new highs despite macro uncertainty, investors may be wondering where to invest their money. Although an overvalued market is risky due to a potential crash or correction, investing in index funds is key for long-term foundational growth.

As a simple guy like Warren Buffett, the Oracle of Omaha, has always recommended investing in an index fund and holding for the long-term. And Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF™ (NYSEARCA:SCHG) is a perfect example.

This is because of their passive investment style and low cost with an expense ratio of just 0.04%. This means you only pay $4 annually for every $10,000 invested, something important if you plan on holding for the foreseeable future.

Moreover, SCHG is also a way to get exposure to Technology (XLK) giants, many whom are overvalued due to the explosive growth of Artificial Intelligence. If you're looking for a low-cost fund for big growth and to build your wealth, consider SCHG.

Previous Buy Case

I last covered SCHG back in July, assigning the ETF a buy rating due to their high growth exposure and potential outperformance. Since, the ETF has beaten the S&P (SP500), up nearly 7% in comparison to 5.56% for the index.

Delivering Big Growth

As mentioned previously, Schwab's U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF is a simple, yet cost-effective way to achieve growth with exposure to AI. With the market expected to continue growing at a high rate, SCHG looks poised to benefit as a result of its high tech exposure.

Currently, SCHG's portfolio mostly consists of technology stocks with the sector making up 48%. What makes the fund unique is their passive investment style, meaning a low cost structure for long-term investors.

Like most ETFs, SCHG also rebalances its portfolio annually. This is a way to get exposure to outperforming companies and reducing exposure to underperforming ones.

Currently, Nvidia Corp (NVDA) is the only company in SCHG's portfolio that has double-digit exposure at 11.30%. The rest of the Magnificent 7 stocks like Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Broadcom (AVGO), and Amazon (AMZN) have high, single-digit exposure.

Broadcom and Nvidia have significantly outperformed the S&P (SP500), up over 92% and 33% respectively. Apple has been the biggest laggard, up single-digits, while Amazon has also been a bit of a disappointment in terms of price appreciation.

But this is the good thing about owning ETFs, especially growth-focused ones like SCHG. Another growth-focused fund I've been researching lately is Invesco's S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO).

A key difference is SPMO looks to track the S&P, while SCHG tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index. Another difference, something I give SPMO an advantage in is that the ETF rebalances twice a year instead of once.

I recently covered this in an article, so I won't go too much in-depth. But this potentially allows the fund to outperform vs other growth-focused peers like SCHG. The latter has a longer track record with an inception date of 12/11/2009 compared to 10/09/2015 for SPMO.

If you compare them from SPMO's inception date, SCHG still managed to slightly outperform SPMO in price returns, up nearly 384% compared to 375%.

Even if you compared to them to peers, who all came before them with the exception of Vanguard's S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SCHG still bested its peer group.

Using VOO's inception date of 09/07/2010, Schwab's U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF still outperformed. Vanguard's Mega Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (MGK) came in a close second, up close to 861% to roughly 870% for SCHG.

In total returns, MGK slightly beats out SCHG, up 1,027.75% to 1,010.02%. VOO lags behind the entire group, up nearly 682%. All solid returns, but SCHG's long-term returns are attractive in comparison. Especially, for a passively-managed fund.

Additionally, SCHG has the lowest expense ratio of the group with the exception of VOO's 0.03%. VUG's is similar at 0.04%, while MGK has the highest of the group at 0.07%.

This, and SCHG's much cheaper share price makes them more appealing for individual investors. On a P/E basis, however, neither fund is cheap due to the market's overall valuation.

The chart below shows the price to earnings and price to book value ratio multiples of each fund from cheapest to most expensive. As you can see, neither is cheap relative to the overall market.

ETF
